video_title: Supreme Court Re-Shapes Power of Presidency | The Close 6/29/2026
gist: Bloomberg's The Close breaks down a landmark Supreme Court ruling that lets the president fire independent-agency heads (FTC/FCC) at will while shielding the Fed for now, and layers on market signal across the AI-infrastructure rally, Fed-on-hold rate views, Comcast's media spinoff and telecom/media M&A, and Goldman's AI job-displacement forecast.

themes.theme: Supreme Court reshaping executive power over agencies

insights.claim: The Supreme Court ruled the president can fire commissioners of independent agencies like the FTC regardless of good cause, rejecting the idea of a fourth branch of government not directly accountable to the president.
insights.speaker: Bill (former FTC chair)
insights.so_what: Independent regulators (FTC, FCC and dozens more) now effectively become extensions of the executive branch, raising regulatory-policy volatility for regulated industries.
insights.quote: THE PRESIDENT MUST HAVE THE POWER TO FIRE COMMISSIONERS REGARDLESS OF WHETHER OR NOT HE HAS GOOD CAUSE.

insights.claim: Agencies will likely abandon the bipartisan multi-member model and shift to single-party representation because nothing compels the president to seat minority commissioners on a timetable.
insights.speaker: Bill (former FTC chair)
insights.so_what: Regulatory decisions become more partisan and swing harder with each administration, increasing policy risk for companies planning multi-year strategies.
insights.quote: WE WILL WALK AWAY IN MANY WAYS FROM HAVING A COMBINATION OF REPUBLICANS AND DEMOCRATS. WE WILL HAVE SINGLE PARTY REPRESENTATION

insights.claim: Stripping agencies of political autonomy weakens their standing before courts because judges no longer defer to decisions that appear driven by political expediency rather than professional expertise.
insights.speaker: Bill (former FTC chair)
insights.so_what: Politicized agency rulings may be more easily overturned in litigation, adding uncertainty to enforcement outcomes companies face.
insights.quote: AS SOON AS IT APPEARS AS THOUGH DECISIONS OF THE PRODUCT OF POLITICAL EXPEDIENCY ARE PRESSURE FROM ELECTED OFFICIALS, THERE IS NO BASIS FOR THE COURT TO RESPECT THE DECISIONS OF THE AGENCY

insights.claim: The Fed ruling rested largely on procedural grounds around how Governor Cook was dismissed, leaving open the possibility that a more carefully constructed case could later expand presidential firing power over the Fed.
insights.speaker: Bill (former FTC chair)
insights.so_what: Fed independence is protected for now but not permanently settled, a lingering tail risk for rate expectations and markets.
insights.quote: IT WAS THE IMPETUOUS AND AS OF THE DISMISSAL THAT I THINK RAISED CONCERNS BY THE COURT

insights.claim: The court appeared reluctant to send markets into a tailspin by dismissing a Fed board member central to the monetary system, suggesting market-stability concerns shaped the Fed carve-out.
insights.speaker: Bill (former FTC chair)
insights.so_what: The judiciary is factoring market fragility into rulings, implying continued protection of Fed independence to avoid disruption.
insights.quote: THE COURT WAS ALSO VERY CONCERNED HERE THAT IT DOES NOT WANT TO SEND THE MARKETS INTO A TAILSPIN SIMPLY BY DISMISSING A MEMBER OF A BOARD

insights.claim: The Fed ruling was 5-4 but roughly eight of nine justices would stand by Fed independence provided a member could still be fired for cause, per a former Fed governor's read.
insights.speaker: Betsy Duke
insights.so_what: Broad justice support means Fed independence is more durable than the narrow vote suggests, a stabilizing signal for rate markets.
insights.quote: IT LOOKS TO ME LIKE EIGHT OF THE JUSTICES WOULD STAND BY FED INDEPENDENCE WITH THE PROVISION THAT A MEMBER COULD BE FIRED FOR CAUSE.

insights.claim: Roberts cited the first and second US attempts to form a central bank as precedent that the founding fathers believed in an independent central bank.
insights.speaker: Betsy Duke
insights.so_what: The originalist grounding makes the pro-independence stance harder to reverse in future cases.
insights.quote: HE REACHED ALL THE WAY BACK TO THE FIRST ATTEMPT TO FORM A CENTRAL BANK

insights.claim: Even with firing protection, sitting Fed governors can still be harassed and dragged through the courts, which is costly for individuals and remains a residual pressure risk.
insights.speaker: Betsy Duke
insights.so_what: Legal harassment is a backdoor channel to pressure Fed officials even if outright dismissal is barred.
insights.quote: YOU MIGHT NOT BE ABLE TO FIRE A MEMBER OF THE BOARD OF GOVERNORS BUT YOU CAN CERTAINLY HARASS THEM AND DRAGGED HIM THROUGH THE COURTS

insights.claim: Jerome Powell is expected to leave the board once the DOJ-suspended investigation is formally closed, with an inspector general report expected by end of summer.
insights.speaker: Betsy Duke
insights.so_what: Clears a potential overhang on Fed leadership continuity; Warsh is referenced as the new Fed chair.
insights.quote: HE PLANS TO LEAVE AS SOON AS HE IS -- IT IS DETERMINED THIS CASE IS TRULY ENDED.

themes.theme: Fed on hold, inflation stickiness and front-end positioning

insights.claim: The Fed is now priced as the most hawkish of the Fed, ECB and BOE, with about 92 basis points of change and its implied-rate line climbing over recent months while ECB and BOE odds fell.
insights.speaker: Katie Greifeld
insights.so_what: Divergent central-bank paths favor the dollar and reprice front-end rate trades.
insights.quote: RIGHT NOW THE FED IS SEEN AS THE MOST HAWKISH OF THOSE THREE. WERE LOOKING AT 92 BASIS POINTS OF CHANGE.

insights.claim: PIMCO forecasts inflation staying above 3% over the medium term, keeping the Fed on hold for the remainder of the year rather than reacting proactively.
insights.speaker: Jerome Schneider
insights.so_what: Cash yielding ~3% underperforms inflation, so investors risk negative real returns holding cash.
insights.quote: IT MIGHT TAKE SEVERAL YEARS WHICH MEANS THE FEDERAL RESERVE WILL PROBABLY BE ON ITS HANDS OVER THE COURSE OF THE NEXT SEVERAL MEETINGS

insights.claim: Investors should move out of the $7.9 trillion sitting in money market funds into a diversified front-end fixed-income portfolio that can pick up 100-200 basis points and produce 5-7% equity-like returns.
insights.speaker: Jerome Schneider
insights.so_what: Actionable rotation call: cash is a losing real trade; short-duration diversified credit offers better risk-adjusted yield.
insights.quote: TO THE SHORT-TERM SPACE WHICH IS PRODUCING FIVE TO 7% RETURNS

insights.claim: Much of the growth in money market funds is organic compounding plus a holding place for capital earmarked for AI pursuits, not necessarily conviction cash allocation.
insights.speaker: Jerome Schneider
insights.so_what: Headline MMF growth overstates true risk-aversion; some is dry powder awaiting deployment.
insights.quote: ALSO SEEING A HOLDING PLACE FOR THE CAPITOL AI PURSUITS AND OTHER PLACES

themes.theme: AI-driven equity rally, concentration and overlap risk

insights.claim: AI infrastructure stocks alone likely contributed 60% of S&P 500 EPS growth for the quarter, per Goldman strategists, and the quarter was the best ever for the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index.
insights.speaker: Romaine Bostick
insights.so_what: Extreme earnings concentration in AI infra means any single miss could disproportionately hit index EPS; RBC raised its 12-month S&P target to 8150.
insights.quote: AS A INFRASTRUCTURE STOCKS ALONE HAVE LIKELY CONTRIBUTED 60% OF S&P EPS GROWTH FOR THE QUARTER

insights.claim: The BIS warned in its annual report that any disappointment in AI-related returns could trigger a pullback in financing that turns the current boom into a protracted bust with larger macroeconomic consequences.
insights.speaker: Romaine Bostick
insights.so_what: Systemic tail risk: AI-financing fragility, amplified by levered ETFs and hedge-fund positioning, could fuel a rapid reversal.
insights.quote: DURING THIS BOOM INTO A PROTRACTED BUST WITH LARGER MACROECONOMIC CONSEQUENCES

insights.claim: There is hidden overlap risk because within the Russell 1000 top-10 stocks account for ~16% of value and ~55% of growth, so investors think they are diversified across value and growth but hold the same names.
insights.speaker: Meera (JPMorgan Asset Management)
insights.so_what: Style diversification is illusory when Mag 7 dominates both value and growth indices; investors are unknowingly doubling down.
insights.quote: PEOPLE THINK THEY HAVE VALUE. I HAVE GROWS. THERE IS OVERLAP RISK HERE

insights.claim: As tech shifts from capital-light to capital-intensive due to AI buildouts, those earnings streams and their ecosystem offshoots become much more cyclical, forcing investors to prepare for cyclicality.
insights.speaker: Meera (JPMorgan Asset Management)
insights.so_what: The traditional treatment of tech as a secular grower breaks down; portfolios need to price in cyclical AI-capex risk.
insights.quote: AS TECH MOVES FROM CAPITAL LIGHT TO CAPITAL INTENSIVE

insights.claim: Financials have strong fundamentals (earnings growth, low credit-card charge-offs, M&A/IPO tailwinds) but weak sentiment and multiple compression, presenting a balanced high-quality value opportunity.
insights.speaker: Meera (JPMorgan Asset Management)
insights.so_what: Financials, the worst-performing S&P sector, may be mispriced given they can take share from a private-credit reckoning as a loan-growth opportunity.
insights.quote: FINANCIALS ARE ABLE TO TAKE THE SHARE IN THE PUBLIC MARKETS FROM PRIVATE CREDIT AS PRIVATE CREDIT GOES THROUGH A LITTLE BIT OF A RECKONING

insights.claim: Just as picking internet winners 30 years ago would mostly have been wrong, today's AI winners may not be the leaders in five to ten years.
insights.speaker: Romaine Bostick
insights.so_what: Cautions against concentrated bets on current AI leaders given historical difficulty of picking durable disruptors.
insights.quote: PICKING THE WINNERS BACK THEN MOST LIKELY WOULD HAVE BEEN WRONG.

insights.claim: Comcast's plan to spin off NBCUniversal and Sky into a separate company creates two pure plays (content and distribution) that each become an acquirer or target, with Comcast more likely to be an acquirer.
insights.speaker: David (Seaport Research Partners)
insights.so_what: The split unlocks strategic flexibility and could catalyze broad connectivity/media consolidation; Comcast stock hit +17% intraday, closing +4.5%, Charter +9.4%.
insights.quote: I THINK COMCAST IS GOING TO BE MORE ACQUIRED.

insights.claim: SpaceX having a public equity that could be used as currency introduces a new variable that could pressure media/telecom targets, especially around the Starlink asset.
insights.speaker: David (Seaport Research Partners)
insights.so_what: SpaceX joining the Nasdaq 100 (July 7) and its equity currency reshape competitive dynamics for cable/telecom incumbents like Verizon and Charter.
insights.quote: YOU DO HAVE THE NEW VARIABLE OF SPACEX HAVING A PUBLIC EQUITY THAT COULD BE USED

insights.claim: Verizon fell 5.2% after reports that SpaceX and Charter held executive-level talks about partnering on a consumer mobile phone offering, plus a projected $700-800 million Q2 loss.
insights.speaker: Tim Stenovec
insights.so_what: A SpaceX-Charter wireless partnership is a direct competitive threat to incumbent carriers.
insights.quote: SHARES FELL AFTER BLOOMBERG NEWS REPORTED THAT SPACEX AND CHARTER HAVE HELD EXECUTIVE-LEVEL TALKS ABOUT PARTNERING ON A CONSUMER MOBILE PHONE OFFERING

insights.claim: Rocket Lab's acquisition of Iridium is primarily about acquiring scarce spectrum needed to compete with Starlink, not just launch capability.
insights.speaker: Norah Mulinda
insights.so_what: Spectrum is the strategic bottleneck for satellite-to-cellphone competition; Rocket Lab is positioning as second to SpaceX but remains far smaller.
insights.quote: WHAT THEY ARE LOOKING FOR OUT OF YOUR IRIDIUM IS SPECTRUM. WHICH IS SCARCE.

themes.theme: London Stock Exchange, data monetization and listings competition

insights.claim: Over 90% of LSEG's data is effectively proprietary, and AI is making that data more valuable because agents and models consume more data than humans through new AI distribution channels.
insights.speaker: David Schwimmer
insights.so_what: AI is a demand tailwind, not a threat, for proprietary financial-data owners; higher machine consumption drives revenue.
insights.quote: AN AGENT OR MODEL TENDS TO CONSUME MORE DATA THAN A HUMAN. I THINK IT'S GOING TO BE A BIG POSITIVE FOR US

insights.claim: UK companies that moved to list and raise capital in the US have a terrible track record: of ~20 that raised over $100M, only 3-4 are still doing well while ~17 were delisted or trade down 60-70% on average.
insights.speaker: David Schwimmer
insights.so_what: Undercuts the narrative that US listings are superior; smaller non-US companies risk becoming orphan stocks in the US.
insights.quote: THE OTHER 17 OR SO IS A COMBINATION OF HAVING BEEN DELISTED OR TRADING DOWN AN AVERAGE OF 60 TO 70%. IT IS A FRANKLY TERRIBLE TRACK RECORD.

insights.claim: LSEG's IPO pipeline is the strongest Schwimmer has seen in eight years, stronger than before the financial crisis, spanning UK, Europe, Middle East and Asia into the back half and next year.
insights.speaker: David Schwimmer
insights.so_what: Signals a genuine revival in London listings despite political instability, a competitive counterpoint to US exchanges.
insights.quote: WE ARE SEEING A HEALTHIER PIPELINE THAN I'VE SEEN IN MY EIGHT YEARS

insights.claim: LSEG targeted a 3 billion pound buyback (below activist Elliott's requested 5 billion) to preserve strategic flexibility while landing at 1.5-2.5x net EBITDA range by year-end.
insights.speaker: David Schwimmer
insights.so_what: Management is balancing activist pressure against M&A optionality, keeping dry powder for its ~7 modest acquisitions cadence.
insights.quote: I THINK THE 3 BILLION BUYBACK THAT WE ANNOUNCED... IT ALSO ALLOWS US TO MAINTAIN OUR STRATEGIC FLEXIBILITY

themes.theme: AI labor displacement and job creation

insights.claim: Goldman estimates AI could displace more than 9% of workers over the full transition (up from an earlier ~6% estimate), driven by AI delivering 15% gross productivity upside after full adoption.
insights.speaker: Joseph Briggs
insights.so_what: Headline displacement is large but spread over ~10 years, implying a manageable 0.5-1.0 point annual rise in unemployment.
insights.quote: A.I. WOULD DELIVER 15% GROSS PRODUCTIVITY UPSIDE FOLLOWING FULL ADOPTION

insights.claim: AI hiring impact is already visible and concentrated in tech, management consulting and graphic design, where hiring over the last couple years has underperformed by around 5%.
insights.speaker: Joseph Briggs
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific sectors already absorbing AI-driven job compression, useful for labor and sector positioning.
insights.quote: HIRING OF THE LAST COUPLE OF YEARS HAS UNDERPERFORMED BY AROUND 5%

insights.claim: 85% of US job growth over the last 85 years has been driven by technology-led creative destruction, and gross job creation runs 25-35 million a year versus ~1 million net.
insights.speaker: Joseph Briggs
insights.so_what: Historical base rate argues AI will accelerate job creation, offsetting displacement if new roles emerge in lockstep.
insights.quote: 85% OF JOB GROWTH OVER THE LAST 85 YEARS HAS BEEN DRIVEN BY THE CREATIVE DESTRUCTION DUE TO TECHNOLOGY

insights.claim: Data center construction is already generating 200,000 to 250,000 jobs, and specific AI use cases show productivity uplift of 20-30% in applications to date.
insights.speaker: Joseph Briggs
insights.so_what: Concrete near-term job creation and micro-level productivity gains, even though macro productivity statistics show little yet.
insights.quote: ALREADY TODAY WE ARE SEEING 200,000 TO 250,000 JOBS EMERGE ON THE BACK OF DATA CENTER CONSTRUCTION

insights.claim: Honeywell Aerospace enters its spinoff with a $90 billion backlog and $19 billion of current-year demand (up 20% year-on-year), with capacity unlock rather than M&A as its top capital priority.
insights.speaker: Jim Currier
insights.so_what: Demand vastly exceeds supply; the investable thesis is supply-base capacity expansion, with M&A second and requiring differentiated tech.
insights.quote: WE HAVE A $90 BILLION IN BACKLOG, $19 BILLION OF DEMAND WE ARE CURRENTLY SEEING TO FULFILL FOR THE YEAR

insights.claim: Rising fuel prices and Middle East conflict have driven an uptick in business-aviation flight hours, an underappreciated Honeywell exposure where half of OEM business is business aviation.
insights.speaker: Jim Currier
insights.so_what: Geopolitical stress that hurts commercial airlines can paradoxically benefit business-aviation-exposed suppliers.
insights.quote: WHAT WE HAVE SEEN AS A RESULT OF THE CONFLICTS AND THE RISING FUEL PRICES IS ACTUALLY AN UPTICK IN FLIGHT HOUR TRAFFIC FOR BUSINESS AVIATION.

insights.claim: Nike's turnaround is going the wrong direction with revenue expected down 2-4% (after flat last quarter), China projected down heavy double digits, and rising discounting signaling future gross-margin pressure.
insights.speaker: Stacey (SW Retail Advisors)
insights.so_what: Two years into the Elliott Hill turnaround, deterioration and heavier direct-channel discounts point to continued margin risk ahead of earnings.
insights.quote: THIS QUARTER THEY WILL BE DOWN 2% TO 4%

insights.claim: A better way to play the World Cup than Nike is Dick's Sporting Goods, which owns every brand and has cleaned up the newly acquired, now largely full-priced Footlocker.
insights.speaker: Stacey (SW Retail Advisors)
insights.so_what: Retailer with broad brand exposure and improved pricing discipline may capture sporting-event demand better than a single struggling brand.
insights.quote: I WOULD LOOK AT DICK'S SPORTING GOODS.

insights.claim: Nike is losing market share to Adidas and Hoka, making its problem fundamentally a product story rather than a financial-management issue despite a new CFO appointment.
insights.speaker: Stacey (SW Retail Advisors)
insights.so_what: Investors should watch product and shelf-allocation execution, not CFO changes, as the key turnaround signal.
insights.quote: THIS IS A PRODUCT STORY. WHEN YOU ARE LOSING MARKET SHARE TO ADIDAS AND HOKA
